one_line_takeaway: Fourteen SOTA LLMs and seven prompting strategies all perform near chance (best 54.5% balanced accuracy) at distinguishing vulnerable C/C++ code from its patched version, and neither scaling model size/data nor fine-tuning fixes it—evidence that vulnerability detection is a code-reasoning problem current autoregressive LLMs cannot solve by scaling alone.
key_finding: No model or prompt beat the 50% random-guess Balanced Accuracy baseline by more than 5 points; the best was StarCoder2 at 54.5% BA. Even GPT-4-turbo could not distinguish a vulnerable function from its patched version in 67.4% of pairs (average across models: 69.7% of pairs indistinguishable). Scaling failed on every axis: no correlation between model size and performance (R2=0.02, p=0.72); doubling training data (StarCoder->StarCoder2) gained only ~4%; instruction fine-tuning gave no substantial change; adapter fine-tuning on PrimeVul made balanced-set performance worse and improved imbalanced-set by only 1.2%. Root-cause error analysis: 44% of responses were error-free, but LLMs misunderstood bounds/NULL checks in 80/159 (50%) of relevant responses, and misread string (10%), arithmetic (8%), pointer (6%), and alloc/free (5%) operations. CoT-Annotations cut bounds/NULL errors by 15-70% for Code LLAMA/MagiCoder/Mistral but did not lift overall detection accuracy, because failures in later multi-step logical-reasoning steps still block correct answers. The authors attribute this to autoregressive pretraining lacking execution semantics.
core_contribution: An empirical study arguing that software vulnerability detection is a hard, multi-step CODE-REASONING task that current LLMs largely fail. The paper's OWN contributions (distinct from prior work): (1) a systematic benchmark of 14 SOTA LLMs across 7 prompting methods on the SVEN dataset, showing all sit near random guessing; (2) three new dataset-metadata-driven prompts (Contrastive pairs, CoT-CVE, CoT-StaticAnalysis) plus a static-analysis-guided CoT-Annotations prototype; (3) a rigorous manual error analysis of 300 responses across 100 programs with a fine-grained, multi-rater error taxonomy tied to reasoning steps; and (4) a 'natural experiment' isolating the effect of model size, code-vs-NL data, data volume, instruction fine-tuning, and adapter fine-tuning, none of which meaningfully help. Prior studies (Ullah et al. 2023; Yu et al. 2024; Nong et al. 2024) are explicitly framed as smaller/coarser and are NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender threat model. The security relevance is defensive tooling: can LLMs serve as automated vulnerability detectors on real C/C++ code? The task is binary classification (vulnerable vs. fixed) over functions covering memory-safety CWEs (buffer overflow / OOB read-write CWE-125/787, null-pointer dereference CWE-476, use-after-free CWE-416, integer overflow CWE-190). The paper's finding is that LLMs are unreliable at this defensive task, which implies caution against deploying them as trustworthy detectors, but the paper does not model an adversary crafting evasive inputs.
limitations: Evaluation is limited to SVEN (386 vuln + 386 fixed C/C++ functions) plus small CWE/SARD examples; findings may not transfer to other languages or repository-level context. Models are as of March 2024 and pre-reasoning-era; the paper itself notes o1/DeepSeek-R1 style reasoning models as untested promising directions. The CoT-Annotations case study is narrow (NPD only, 198 responses, single-rater per response due to time constraints, max 25 responses/model). Manual error analysis (300 responses) is subjective despite the multi-rater protocol. Table 1's cross-domain scores are pulled from external leaderboards/model papers rather than re-measured, and there is a minor internal inconsistency (LLAMA 2 listed as 34b in Table 1 vs 7B/13B in the model table).

datasets_used: SVEN (He & Vechev 2023): 386 vulnerable functions + their fixed versions = 772 real-world C/C++ programs, avg 168 lines, ~94% label accuracy (primary evaluation set)
Simple CWE/SARD examples (MITRE CWE and NIST SARD): ~25 lines/function, used as an easier control set
PrimeVul (Ding et al. 2025): used only for adapter fine-tuning of StarCoder2-7B
Big-Vul (Fan et al. 2020): CVE bug reports used to build CoT-CVE in-context examples
D2A (Zheng et al. 2021): Infer static-analyzer buggy paths used to build CoT-StaticAnalysis examples
quantitative_results: Best model overall: StarCoder2 = 54.5% Balanced Accuracy on SVEN (random baseline = 50%)
No model/prompt exceeded random by more than 5% BA

Per-model SVEN BA (Table 1): StarChat 50.9, MagiCoder 50.9, LLAMA 2 51.2, Mistral 51.4, GPT-3.5-turbo 51.8, Mixtral 51.9, DeepSeek 52.1, Gemini 52.1, StarCoder 52.2, WizardCoder 52.4, Code LLAMA 52.6, GPT-4-turbo 52.9, StarChat2 53.6, StarCoder2 54.5
GPT-4-turbo could not distinguish vulnerable vs fixed in 67.4% of pairs; average across all models = 69.7% indistinguishable; best (WizardCoder) still 55.0% indistinguishable
Simple CWE examples: 42-67% BA across models (e.g., DeepSeek 66.67, GPT-4 65.78, LLAMA 2 46.49, StarCoder 41.52)
Model size vs performance: R2 = 0.02, p = 0.72 (no correlation)
Adapter fine-tuning on PrimeVul: worse on balanced data, +1.2% on imbalanced data

Error analysis (300 responses, 100 programs): 44% error-free; bounds/NULL check misunderstood 80/159 (50%); string op 3/29 (10%); arithmetic op 8/96 (8%); pointer op 9/147 (6%); alloc/free 4/81 (5%); index op 1/60 (2%); logical-reasoning errors ~9%; hallucination/repetition ~10%
CoT-Annotations reduced bounds/NULL check errors by 15-70% for Code LLAMA, MagiCoder, Mistral, but they still missed 23-67% of checks and overall detection did not improve
baselines_compared: In-context n-shot: random selection and RAG/CodeBERT-embedding retrieval
CoT-Annotations prompt (paper's own; NPD case study)
Cross-domain reference scores (HumanEval code-gen, CruxEval code-execution, GSM8k math, CommonsenseQA NL reasoning) used to contrast with vuln-detection performance
